text: The surgeon begins the entry dissection at the inferior pulmonary ligament, retracting the lung anteriorly and cutting the ligament to define the inferior pulmonary vein. Tiny lymph nodes are visible as part of the specimen. The assistant provides traction by pulling the lung, while the surgeon applies counter traction by retracting the specimen posteriorly. An avascular plane is visible between the inferior pulmonary vein and the specimen, with fibrofatty tissue included in the specimen. Sharp dissection is performed using a monopolar hook, maintaining a dry surgical field.

The surgeon performs mesoesophageal dissection, taking all fibrofatty tissue with the specimen while preserving important anatomical landmarks. The dissection proceeds along the pericardium up to the hiatus, ensuring the nodal basin above the hiatus is addressed. The contralateral pleura becomes visible, marking the limit of the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the inferior pulmonary vein, safeguarding it while addressing lymph node basins situated above and below it on the left side. The pleura over the right bronchus is incised, exposing the area for further dissection. Lymph nodes at station 108 are removed from the right bronchus along with the specimen. The assistant retracts the right bronchus downward anteriorly, while the surgeon provides counter traction by lifting tissue posteriorly, revealing a clear dissection plane. The vagus nerve to the bronchus is preserved, with its branches to the esophagus selectively cut. The right bronchial artery is visualized, and its branches to the esophagus are selectively cut.

The surgeon separates the esophagus from the trachea, staying flush to the esophagus and dissecting parallel to it. The left border of the trachea is visible, serving as a landmark for dissection. The left bronchus is defined and delineated. After completing dissection along the tracheobronchial tree, the surgeon switches to posterior dissection, staying flush to the azygous vein and making a posterior cut on the mediastinal pleura. Fibrofatty tissue is removed from the azygous vein, and the prevascular fascia is dissected, revealing the descending aorta. Fat containing the thoracic duct is lifted away from the descending aorta and azygous vein, allowing for thoracic duct clipping and cutting. The posterior dissection continues, removing tissue from the descending aorta.

The surgeon completes the dissection up to the contralateral pleura, taking station 112 lymph nodes with the specimen. A clear plane of separation is visible between the mesoesophagus and the left pleura. If the left pleura is accidentally opened, the window is enlarged at the end of the thoracoscopic phase to avoid placing a contralateral intercostal drainage tube. The procedure then transitions to supra-axiacus dissection, focusing on the right side.

The surgeon performs recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection by first defining the superior vena cava and making a pleural incision along it, reaching the apex of the chest to identify the right subclavian artery. The vagus nerve, subclavian artery, and right recurrent laryngeal nerve are visible, with the latter encircling the subclavian artery. The specimen is separated from the trachea, and dissection proceeds towards the neck, with careful use of harmonic energy to avoid injury to the trachea. The right side of the vagus nerve is dissected, revealing the station 106 tracheobronchial lymph node between the trachea and vagus nerve.

The surgeon carefully dissects the right recurrent laryngeal lymph node group, using a combination of sharp dissection and scissors to define the nerve fibers. The right recurrent laryngeal nerve is clearly visible, and fibro-fatty tissue is removed with the lymph node packet. The dissection is performed with caution to avoid thermal injury, and the upper limit of the dissection is marked by the inferior thyroid artery.

The surgeon completes the right recurrent dissection, removing the specimen en masse with station 106 recurrent lymph node. The procedure then transitions to the left side recurrent dissection, where the specimen is separated from the left side of the trachea. The tracheal dip maneuver is used to remove fibro fatty tissue from the trachea, exposing the visceral fascia covering the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node. The left recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible, and the AP window lymph node is gradually separated from the trachea and main pulmonary artery. The AP window lymph nodes are arranged in a single file pattern, requiring careful removal to ensure all nodes are extracted.

The surgeon performs suprazygous lymph node dissection, with the descending aorta visible posteriorly and the trachea anteriorly. The cleared AP window lymph nodes are attached to the specimen. Both the left and right recurrent laryngeal nerves are visible. The final view shows the surgical field at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon performs an intrathoracic minimal invasive Ivor Lewis esophagectomy with intrathoracic anastomosis. The esophagus is prepared for anastomosis by separating lymph nodes and pulling the specimen from the abdomen. The thoracic phase begins with port placement, and the esophagus is transected. A utility incision is made in the seventh intercostal space to extract the specimen. The gastric conduit is prepared using staplers, and a side-to-side stapled anastomosis is performed. The final steps include applying endoGI staplers for the anastomosis and placing a negative suction drain to manage the conduit.
